heading: MODULUS DECIMUSSEPTIMUS
card_summary: Modulus Decimusseptimus opens the seventeenth module of the Eighth Tone with the single-word rubric "Unio" and the heading "De beatorum unione cum Deo" — on the union of the blessed with God. Zorzi anchors the discussion in Christ's high-priestly prayer from the Gospel of John (chapters 17), citing the words "Pater sancte serva eos ... ut sint unum, sicut et nos" and the intercessory passage for all future believers "ut omnes unum sint, sicut tu Pater in me, et ego in te." He then turns to Plotinus as a philosophical witness, quoting the Neoplatonist on the secrecy surrounding mystical initiation and the prohibition against disclosing divine things to the uninitiated. The module invokes Parmenides's doctrine that "all things are one," defended against Aristotelian criticism through the voice of the commentator Simplicius, who argues that Aristotle argued against the words, not the meaning, of Parmenides. The processio-and-return schema underlies the whole: all things proceed from the One and must strip away multiplicity — imagination, discursive reason, sense — to return to it.
essay:
# MODULUS DECIMUSSEPTIMUS — "De beatorum unione cum Deo"

## Title and Translation

*Modulus Decimusseptimus* is the seventeenth modulus (module or measure) within the Eighth Tone (*Tonus Octavus*) of Francesco Zorzi's *De Harmonia Mundi* (Venice 1525; Italian edition ed. Saverio Campanini, Bompiani 2010). The single-word rubric that immediately follows the title is **Unio** — Union — and the full descriptive heading reads *De beatorum unione cum Deo, Concentus Unicus*: "On the union of the blessed with God, a single harmony." The musical metaphors of *tonus*, *modulus*, and *concentus* are characteristic of Zorzi's organising conceit throughout the work: sacred doctrine is arranged as a cosmic musical score.

## What the Section Introduces

This title page signals that the seventeenth module will be devoted entirely to the doctrine of mystical union — the eschatological and contemplative merging of the sanctified soul with the divine. The rubric *Concentus Unicus* ("a single harmony") implies that the union to be discussed is not merely moral or intellectual but ontological: a collapse of multiplicity back into the primordial One.

## Opening Movement

Zorzi begins immediately with the scriptural warrant: Christ's intercessory prayer in John 17, the petition "that they may be one, as we are one" (*ut sint unum, sicut et nos*). The verse is read not as a metaphor for ecclesial solidarity but as a disclosure of the very *modus* — the manner — of divine union. Christ's words "as you, Father, are in me and I in you" supply the ontological template.

Zorzi then summons Plotinus as the supreme pagan witness to this same mystery, quoting him on the duty of silence surrounding initiation into the highest realities. The invocation of Parmenides — defended by Simplicius against Aristotle's merely verbal objections — establishes the Neoplatonic metaphysical frame: all things proceed from the One and are drawn by necessity to return, shedding the veils of imagination, discursive reason, and sense that Aristotle never transcended.

The reader can expect the body of this module to develop the theology of deification (*unio mystica*) in dialogue with Plotinian henology, Kabbalistic *devekut*, and patristic sources, pursuing Zorzi's signature strategy of harmonising Christian revelation with prisca sapientia.
